4.reason: Incorrect argument type for scanf: '&s' passes the address of the array, but scanf expects a char pointer. It should be 's'.

reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-1: 0.16666666666666666 , reason:  [Error: scanf("%s", s) should be used instead of scanf("%s", &s) because s is already a pointer to the first element of the array; using &s passes the address of the array, which is technically the same address but of a different type, leading to undefined behavior or compiler warnings.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-chat-2: 0.16666666666666666 , reason:  [Error: scanf("%s", s) should be used instead of scanf("%s", &s) because s is already an array name that decays to a pointer; using &s is incorrect and may cause undefined behavior]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-1: 0.16666666666666666 , reason:  [Incorrect: '&s' is the address of the entire array, not a pointer to char. Should be 'scanf("%s", s);'. This may cause undefined behavior or compilation warnings.]
reasons.buggy-deepseek-reasoner-2: 0.09090909090909091 , reason:  Error: scanf expects a pointer to char array, not pointer to array; use s instead of &s.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-1: 0.5 , reason:  Incorrect argument type for scanf: '&s' passes the address of the array, but scanf expects a char pointer. It should be 's'.
reasons.buggy-qwen3.6-plus-2: 0.5 , reason:  Type mismatch: '&s' is a pointer to an array, but scanf expects a 'char *'. This causes a compilation warning/error.
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-1: 0.16666666666666666 , reason:  [Passing &s (char (*)[1145]) to scanf with %s expects char*, should be s instead of &s]
reasons.buggy-qwen3-max-2: 0.16666666666666666 , reason:  [The format specifier "%s" expects a char*, but &s is of type char(*)[1145]; should pass 's' instead of '&s']
